one_line_takeaway: SafeAgent moves LLM-agent prompt-injection defense from single-turn text filtering to stateful runtime governance—a Controller mediating the agent loop plus a context-aware decision Core—and empirically beats Llama Guard / LLM Guard on ASB and drives InjecAgent data-stealing attacks to zero while keeping benign utility competitive, at the cost of substantial compute overhead.
key_finding: On ASB, SafeAgent attains the lowest ASR across all three attack classes—DPI 0.4186, IPI 0.2936, MP 0.1858 (vs. undefended 0.7627 / 0.8054 / 0.4485)—while matching LLM Guard's benign PNA of 0.7451, yielding the highest NRP under every attack (DPI 0.4332, IPI 0.5263, MP 0.6067). On InjecAgent it cuts direct-harm ASR to 0.3157 and completely suppresses two-stage data-stealing (S1 = 0.0, S2 = 0.0, total ASR = 0.0). Text-level guardrails are markedly weaker: LLM Guard gives only marginal ASB protection (close to no-defense), and Llama Guard reduces attacks but still leaks in InjecAgent's second stage. Ablations show design choices, not just detection, set the operating point: override confidence 0.0 (conservative rejection) is best for benign PNA (0.7843) and memory poisoning, while 1.0 (repair-oriented) is best for the locally-repairable DPI/IPI attacks; safety-first policy lowers ASR everywhere but collapses PNA to 0.5490, whereas task-first preserves PNA 0.7451 and higher NRP.
core_contribution: SafeAgent is a two-level runtime security architecture for LLM agents that reframes prompt-injection defense as a stateful decision problem over evolving interaction trajectories rather than single-turn input-output filtering. It separates (1) a Runtime Controller—a governed middleware layer that wraps the ReAct agent loop, mediates all tool calls through a controlled boundary, manages a two-layer session context (auditable trace + runtime snapshot), and applies four inspection hooks (user-input, model-plan, observation, model-output) that can allow/block/repair each step; from (2) the SafeAgent Core—a purely semantic, stateful decision module formalized as a 'context-aware Advanced Machine Intelligence (AMI)' (an adaptation of LeCun's AMI decomposition, ref [5], not the authors' own invention) with latent encoding Φ, predictive model Ψ, policy Π, and state update M. The Core is instantiated through five security-specific operators: risk encoding (parallel specialist encoders for obfuscation, memory poisoning, secret leakage, control hijacking, task drift, etc.), advantage–cost modeling, world-model simulation of action consequences, policy arbitration under constrained optimization, and multi-scale memory synchronization (immediate/task-level/long-horizon risk). The Controller also provides six recovery strategies: context override/sanitization, agent replanning, context rollback to a trusted checkpoint, session termination/safe-mode, tool-argument rewriting, and human-in-the-loop / shadow execution.
threat_model: Prompt-injection attacks against ReAct-style tool-using LLM agents, where malicious instructions enter not only via direct user input but also via retrieved documents, tool outputs, and persistent memory that get elevated from data into control input across a multi-step reasoning–action loop. Evaluated attack channels: direct prompt injection (DPI, payload appended to user input), indirect prompt injection (IPI, payload in external tool observations), and memory poisoning (MP, malicious assistant message inserted before the user turn). Attacker-controlled tools are placed in the agent's toolset so injections can trigger real side effects; InjecAgent additionally covers multi-stage data-stealing (extraction then exfiltration). An attack is counted successful (ASR) whenever any attacker tool is invoked.
limitations: The authors state SafeAgent incurs non-trivial computational overhead (a heavy 20B-parameter reasoning Core queried at every inspection hook), which is called an important limitation; latency reduction in the internal decision loop is left to future work. Prompt injection is explicitly 'not fully solved,' and emerging threat classes such as skill injection are noted as requiring further study. Beyond the paper's own statements: evaluation uses a single backbone/Core pair (DeepSeek-V3.2 + gpt-oss-safeguard-20b) and only two guardrail baselines, with no comparison to the system-level defenses (instruction hierarchy, tool-dependency graphs, memory isolation) surveyed in related work; the perfect 0.0 InjecAgent scores and the anomalous Llama Guard aggregate (total 0.1691 below its own per-stage rates) are not reconciled; and the Core operators (world-model simulation, advantage–cost modeling) are described abstractly without concrete implementations or per-operator ablation.

datasets_used: Agent Security Bench (ASB) — 51 agent tasks × 40 attack tools = 2040 evaluation cases (ref [19])
quantitative_results: ASB SafeAgent ASR: DPI 0.4186, IPI 0.2936, MP 0.1858 (all lowest among compared methods)
ASB SafeAgent PNA 0.7451; NRP: DPI 0.4332, IPI 0.5263, MP 0.6067 (highest NRP under all attacks)
ASB No-Defense ASR: DPI 0.7627, IPI 0.8054, MP 0.4485 (PNA 0.8431, highest benign)
ASB Llama Guard ASR: DPI 0.5510, IPI 0.6858, MP 0.4186 (PNA 0.7843)
ASB LLM Guard ASR: DPI 0.7588, IPI 0.7941, MP 0.4426 (PNA 0.7451; only marginal protection)
InjecAgent SafeAgent: Direct Harm 0.3157, S1 0.0000, S2 0.0000, Total 0.0000
InjecAgent No-Defense: Direct Harm 0.8608, S1 0.9338, S2 0.9921, Total 0.9265
InjecAgent Llama Guard: Direct Harm 0.4314, S1 0.2279, S2 0.7419, Total 0.1691
InjecAgent LLM Guard: Direct Harm 0.8431, S1 0.9301, S2 0.9308, Total 0.8658
Override-confidence ablation (ASB): conf 0.0 gives PNA 0.7843 and best MP (ASR 0.1750); conf 1.0 gives best DPI (ASR 0.3701) and IPI (ASR 0.2733)
Policy ablation (ASB): safety-first PNA 0.5490 with lower ASR (DPI 0.2114, IPI 0.1779, MP 0.1270); task-first PNA 0.7451 with higher NRP
